documents.title: Option: boot.bootspec.enableValidation
documents.content: Whether to enable the validation of bootspec documents for each build. This will introduce Go in the build-time closure as we are relying on Cuelang for schema validation. Enable this option if you want to ascertain that your documents are correct .

documents.title: Option: boot.bootspec.extensions
documents.content:
User-defined data that extends the bootspec document.
To reduce incompatibility and prevent names from clashing between applications, it is highly recommended to use a unique namespace for your extensions.

documents.title: Option: boot.bootspec.package

documents.title: Option: boot.extraModulePackages

documents.title: Option: boot.initrd.availableKernelModules
documents.content: The set of kernel modules in the initial ramdisk used during the boot process. This set must include all modules necessary for mounting the root device. That is, it should include modules for the physical device (e.g., SCSI drivers) and for the file system (e.g., ext3). The set specified here is automatically closed under the module dependency relation, i.e., all dependencies of the modules list here are included automatically. The modules listed here are available in the initrd, but are only loaded on demand (e.g., the ext3 module is loaded automatically when an ext3 filesystem is mounted, and modules for PCI devices are loaded when they match the PCI ID of a device in your system). To force a module to be loaded, include it in {option}boot.initrd.kernelModules.

documents.title: Option: boot.initrd.compressor
documents.content:
The compressor to use on the initrd image. May be any of:
The name of one of the predefined compressors, see {file}pkgs/build-support/kernel/initrd-compressor-meta.nix for the definitions.
A function which, given the nixpkgs package set, returns the path to a compressor tool, e.g. pkgs: "${pkgs.pigz}/bin/pigz"
(not recommended, because it does not work when cross-compiling) the full path to a compressor tool, e.g. "${pkgs.pigz}/bin/pigz"

The given program should read data from stdin and write it to stdout compressed.

documents.title: Option: boot.initrd.compressorArgs
documents.content: Arguments to pass to the compressor for the initrd image, or null to use the compressor's defaults.

documents.title: Option: boot.initrd.contents
documents.content: Contents of the initrd.

documents.title: Option: boot.initrd.contents.*.source
documents.content: This option has no description.

documents.title: Option: boot.initrd.contents.*.target
documents.content: This option has no description.

documents.title: Option: boot.initrd.emergencyAccess
documents.content:
Set to true for unauthenticated emergency access to the initramfs rescue shell, and false or null for no access.
Can also be set to a hashed super user password to allow authenticated access to the rescue mode.
When access is denied, finix prints the failure reason on console and reboots after 10s instead of opening a shell.

documents.title: Option: boot.kernelPackages
documents.content:
This option allows you to override the Linux kernel used by NixOS. Since things like external kernel module packages are tied to the kernel you're using, it also overrides those. This option is a function that takes Nixpkgs as an argument (as a convenience), and returns an attribute set containing at the very least an attribute {var}kernel. Additional attributes may be needed depending on your configuration. For instance, if you use the NVIDIA X driver, then it also needs to contain an attribute {var}nvidia_x11.
Please note that we strictly support kernel versions that are maintained by the Linux developers only. More information on the availability of kernel versions is documented in the Linux section of the manual.

documents.title: Option: fileSystems
documents.content:
The file systems to be mounted. It must include an entry for the root directory (mountPoint = "/"). Each entry in the list is an attribute set with the following fields: mountPoint, device, fsType (a file system type recognised by {command}mount; defaults to "auto"), and options (the mount options passed to {command}mount using the {option}-o flag; defaults to [ "defaults" ]).
Instead of specifying device, you can also specify a volume label (label) for file systems that support it, such as ext2/ext3 (see {command}mke2fs -L).

documents.title: Option: fileSystems.<name>.depends
documents.content: List of paths that should be mounted before this one. This filesystem's {option}device and {option}mountPoint are always checked and do not need to be included explicitly. If a path is added to this list, any other filesystem whose mount point is a parent of the path will be mounted before this filesystem. The paths do not need to actually be the {option}mountPoint of some other filesystem.

documents.title: Option: fileSystems.<name>.noCheck

documents.title: Option: finit.run.<name>.remain
documents.content:
By default, a run or task will re-run each time its runlevel is entered, and its post: script does not run on completion.
With remain:yes, the task runs once and does not re-run on runlevel. The post: script will run if the task is explicitly stopped or when the task leaves its valid runlevels.

documents.title: Option: finit.services.<name>.reload
documents.content:
Some services do not support SIGHUP but may have other ways to update the configuration of a running daemon. When reload is defined it is preferred over SIGHUP. Like systemd, finit sets ``$MAINPIDas a convenience to scripts, which in effect also allow settingreload` to `kill -HUP $MAINPID`.
::: {.note} reload is called as PID 1, without any timeout! Meaning, it is up to you to ensure the script is not blocking for seconds at a time or never terminates. :::

documents.title: Option: finit.sysv.<name>.reload
documents.content:

::: {.note} reload is called as PID 1, without any timeout! Meaning, it is up to you to ensure the script is not blocking for seconds at a time or never terminates. :::

documents.title: Option: finit.sysv.<name>.restart_sec
documents.content: The number of seconds before Finit tries to restart a crashing service, default: 2 seconds for the first five retries, then back-off to 5 seconds. The maximum of this configured value and the above (2 and 5) will be used.

documents.title: Option: finit.ttys.<name>.noclear
documents.content: Disables clearing the TTY after each session. Clearing the TTY when a user logs out is usually preferable.

documents.title: Option: finit.ttys.<name>.nologin
documents.content: Disables getty and /bin/login, and gives the user a root (login) shell on the given TTY device immediately. Needless to say, this is a rather insecure option, but can be very useful for developer builds, during board bringup, or similar.

documents.title: Option: finit.ttys.<name>.notty
documents.content: No device node mode. This is insecure and intended only for board bringup or testing scenarios.

documents.title: Option: finit.ttys.<name>.nowait
documents.content: Disables the press Enter to activate console message before actually starting the getty program.

documents.title: Option: finit.ttys.<name>.rescue
documents.content: Start sulogin instead of a regular shell, requiring the root password. Useful for rescue/single-user mode.

documents.title: Option: finit.ttys.<name>.runlevels

documents.title: Option: finit.ttys.<name>.term

documents.title: Option: fonts.enableDefaultPackages
documents.content: Enable a basic set of fonts providing several styles and families and reasonable coverage of Unicode.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.allowBitmaps
documents.content: Allow bitmap fonts. Set to false to ban all bitmap fonts.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.allowType1
documents.content: Allow Type-1 fonts. Default is false because of poor rendering.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.antialias
documents.content: Enable font antialiasing. At high resolution (> 200 DPI), antialiasing has no visible effect; users of such displays may want to disable this option.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.cache32Bit
documents.content: Generate system fonts cache for 32-bit applications.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.defaultFonts.emoji
documents.content:
System-wide default emoji font(s). Multiple fonts may be listed in case a font does not support all emoji.
Note that fontconfig matches color emoji fonts preferentially, so if you want to use a black and white font while having a color font installed (eg. Noto Color Emoji installed alongside Noto Emoji), fontconfig will still choose the color font even when it is later in the list.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.defaultFonts.monospace
documents.content: System-wide default monospace font(s). Multiple fonts may be listed in case multiple languages must be supported.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.defaultFonts.sansSerif
documents.content: System-wide default sans serif font(s). Multiple fonts may be listed in case multiple languages must be supported.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.defaultFonts.serif
documents.content: System-wide default serif font(s). Multiple fonts may be listed in case multiple languages must be supported.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.enable
documents.content: If enabled, a Fontconfig configuration file will be built pointing to a set of default fonts. If you don't care about running X11 applications or any other program that uses Fontconfig, you can leave this option off and prevent a dependency on all those fonts.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.hinting.autohint
documents.content: Enable the autohinter in place of the default interpreter. The results are usually lower quality than correctly-hinted fonts, but better than unhinted fonts.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.hinting.enable
documents.content: Enable font hinting. Hinting aligns glyphs to pixel boundaries to improve rendering sharpness at low resolution. At high resolution (> 200 dpi) hinting will do nothing (at best); users of such displays may want to disable this option.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.hinting.style
documents.content:
Hintstyle is the amount of font reshaping done to line up to the grid.
slight will make the font more fuzzy to line up to the grid but will be better in retaining font shape, while full will be a crisp font that aligns well to the pixel grid but will lose a greater amount of font shape.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.includeUserConf
documents.content: Include the user configuration from {file}~/.config/fontconfig/fonts.conf or {file}~/.config/fontconfig/conf.d.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.localConf
documents.content: System-wide customization file contents, has higher priority than defaultFonts settings.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.subpixel.lcdfilter
documents.content: FreeType LCD filter. At high resolution (> 200 DPI), LCD filtering has no visible effect; users of such displays may want to select none.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.subpixel.rgba
documents.content: Subpixel order. The overwhelming majority of displays are rgb in their normal orientation. Select vrgb for mounting such a display 90 degrees clockwise from its normal orientation or vbgr for mounting 90 degrees counter-clockwise. Select bgr in the unlikely event of mounting 180 degrees from the normal orientation. Reverse these directions in the improbable event that the display's native subpixel order is bgr.

documents.title: Option: fonts.fontconfig.useEmbeddedBitmaps
documents.content: Use embedded bitmaps in fonts like Calibri.

documents.title: Option: fonts.packages
documents.content: List of primary font packages.

documents.title: Option: hardware.console.binaryKeyMap
documents.content: Binary keymap file. If unset then this is generated from the hardware.console.keyMap option.

documents.title: Option: hardware.console.enable
documents.content: Whether to configure the console at boot.

documents.title: Option: hardware.console.keyMap
documents.content: The keyboard mapping table for the virtual consoles. This option may have no effect if hardware.console.binaryKeyMap is set.

documents.title: Option: hardware.console.setvesablank
documents.content: Turn VESA screen blanking on or off.

documents.title: Option: hardware.firmware
documents.content: List of packages containing firmware files. Such files will be loaded automatically if the kernel asks for them (i.e., when it has detected specific hardware that requires firmware to function). If multiple packages contain firmware files with the same name, the first package in the list takes precedence. Note that you must rebuild your system if you add files to any of these directories.

documents.title: Option: hardware.graphics.enable
documents.content:
Whether to enable hardware accelerated graphics drivers.
This is required to allow most graphical applications and environments to use hardware rendering, video encode/decode acceleration, etc.
This option should be enabled by default by the corresponding modules, so you do not usually have to set it yourself.

documents.title: Option: hardware.graphics.enable32Bit
documents.content: On 64-bit systems, whether to also install 32-bit drivers for 32-bit applications (such as Wine).

documents.title: Option: hardware.graphics.extraPackages
documents.content:
Additional packages to add to the default graphics driver lookup path. This can be used to add OpenCL drivers, VA-API/VDPAU drivers, etc.
::: {.note} intel-media-driver supports hardware Broadwell (2014) or newer. Older hardware should use the mostly unmaintained intel-vaapi-driver driver. :::

documents.title: Option: hardware.graphics.extraPackages32
documents.content:
Additional packages to add to 32-bit graphics driver lookup path on 64-bit systems. Used when {option}enable32Bit is set. This can be used to add OpenCL drivers, VA-API/VDPAU drivers, etc.
::: {.note} intel-media-driver supports hardware Broadwell (2014) or newer. Older hardware should use the mostly unmaintained intel-vaapi-driver driver. :::

documents.title: Option: hardware.i2c.enable
documents.content: Whether to enable support for i2c devices. Access to these devices is granted to users in the {option}hardware.i2c.group group.

documents.title: Option: hardware.i2c.group
documents.content:
Group to own the /dev/i2c-* devices.
::: {.note} If you want non-root users to be able to access these i2c devices, add them to this group. :::
::: {.note} If left as the default value this group will automatically be created on system activation, otherwise you are responsible for ensuring the group exists before system activation has completed. :::

documents.title: Option: hardware.nvidia.enable
documents.content: Whether to enable NVIDIA driver support.

documents.title: Option: hardware.nvidia.gsp.enable
documents.content: Whether to enable the GPU System Processor (GSP) on the video card.

documents.title: Option: hardware.nvidia.kernelModule
documents.content: Which NVIDIA kernel module to use: the "open" source GPU kernel modules (recommended on Turing and later GPUs, required on the newest GPUs) or the "closed" proprietary ones.

documents.title: Option: hardware.nvidia.modesetting.enable
documents.content:
Whether to enable kernel modesetting when using the NVIDIA proprietary driver.
Enabling this can fix screen tearing. This is not enabled by default because it is not officially supported by NVIDIA and would not work with SLI.
Enabling this and using version 545 or newer of the proprietary NVIDIA driver causes it to provide its own framebuffer device, which can cause Wayland compositors to work when they otherwise wouldn't.

documents.title: Option: hardware.nvidia.package
documents.content: The NVIDIA driver package to use.

documents.title: Option: hardware.nvidia.powerManagement.enable
documents.content: Whether to enable experimental power management through systemd. For more information, see the NVIDIA docs, on Chapter 21. Configuring Power Management Support.

documents.title: Option: hardware.nvidia.powerManagement.kernelSuspendNotifier
documents.content: Whether to enable NVIDIA driver support for kernel suspend notifiers, which allows the driver to be notified of suspend and resume events by the kernel, rather than relying on systemd services. Requires NVIDIA driver version 595 or newer, and the open source kernel modules.

documents.title: Option: hardware.nvidia.reduceTearing
documents.content:
Reduce screen tearing on displays connected directly to the NVIDIA GPU by enabling its full composition pipeline. This sets the ForceFullCompositionPipeline metamode and, alongside it, disables indirect GLX and enables triple buffering on the X screen.
Trade-offs: it forces the screen to be redrawn even when nothing changed, which can reduce the performance of OpenGL/fullscreen applications (and has been reported to cause issues with WebGL), and it increases the time the driver takes to clock down after load.
See https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/NVIDIA/Troubleshooting#Avoid_screen_tearing_on_Xorg.

documents.title: Option: hardware.nvidia.videoAcceleration
documents.content: Whether to enable video acceleration (VA-API).

documents.title: Option: hardware.uinput.enable
documents.content: Whether to enable uinput support.

documents.title: Option: hardware.uinput.group
documents.content:
Group to own the uinput devices.
::: {.note} If you want non-root users to be able to access these uinput devices, add them to this group. :::
::: {.note} If left as the default value this group will automatically be created on system activation, otherwise you are responsible for ensuring the group exists before system activation has completed. :::

documents.title: Option: i18n.defaultLocale
documents.content: The default locale. It determines the language for program messages, the format for dates and times, sort order, and so on. It also determines the character set, such as UTF-8.

documents.title: Option: i18n.extraLocaleSettings
documents.content: A set of additional system-wide locale settings other than LANG which can be configured with {option}i18n.defaultLocale.

documents.title: Option: i18n.glibcLocales
documents.content:
Customized pkg.glibcLocales package.
Changing this option can disable handling of i18n.defaultLocale and supportedLocale.

documents.title: Option: i18n.supportedLocales
documents.content: List of locales that the system should support. The value "all" means that all locales supported by Glibc will be installed. A full list of supported locales can be found at https://sourceware.org/git/?p=glibc.git;a=blob;f=localedata/SUPPORTED.

documents.title: Option: meta.maintainers
documents.content:
List of maintainers of each module. This option should be defined at most once per module.
The option value is not a list of maintainers, but an attribute set that maps module file names to lists of maintainers.

documents.title: Option: networking.hostId
documents.content:
The 32-bit host ID of the machine, formatted as 8 hexadecimal characters.
You should try to make this ID unique among your machines. You can generate a random 32-bit ID using the following commands:
head -c 8 /etc/machine-id
(this derives it from the machine-id that systemd generates) or
head -c4 /dev/urandom | od -A none -t x4
The primary use case is to ensure when using ZFS that a pool isn't imported accidentally on a wrong machine.

documents.title: Option: networking.hostName
documents.content: The hostname of this system.

documents.title: Option: networking.hosts
documents.content: Locally defined maps of hostnames to IP addresses.

documents.title: Option: nixpkgs.pkgs
documents.content: The nixpkgs package set to use for this system.

documents.title: Option: programs.bash.enable
documents.content: Whether to enable bash.

documents.title: Option: programs.bash.package
documents.content: The package to use for bash.

documents.title: Option: programs.brightnessctl.enable
documents.content: Whether to enable brightnessctl.

documents.title: Option: programs.brightnessctl.package
documents.content: The package to use for brightnessctl.

documents.title: Option: programs.coreutils.package
documents.content:
Package providing the standard core utilities used by the system.
Most modules should use this option instead of depending directly on pkgs.coreutils, allowing alternative implementations such as uutils, busybox, or toybox to be selected globally.

documents.title: Option: programs.dma.enable
documents.content: Whether to enable dma.

documents.title: Option: programs.dma.package
documents.content: The package to use for dma.

documents.title: Option: programs.dma.settings
documents.content: dma configuration. See {manpage}dma(8) for additional details.

documents.title: Option: programs.doas.enable
documents.content: Whether to enable doas.

documents.title: Option: programs.doas.keepEnv
documents.content: Whether or not to keep users in the wheel group's environments during privilege escalation.

documents.title: Option: programs.doas.package
documents.content: The package to use for doas.

documents.title: Option: programs.doas.persist
documents.content: Whether or not to allow credentials to persist for 5 minutes for users in the wheel group.

documents.title: Option: programs.doas.requirePassword
documents.content: Whether or not to prompt the users in the wheel group for their password.

documents.title: Option: programs.fish.enable
documents.content: Whether to enable fish.

documents.title: Option: providers.scheduler.tasks.<name>.interval
documents.content:
The interval at which this task should run its specified {option}command. Accepts either a standard {manpage}crontab(5) expression or one of: hourly, daily, weekly, monthly, or yearly.
If a standard {manpage}crontab(5) expression is provided this value will be passed directly to the scheduler implementation and execute exactly as specified.
If one of the special values, hourly, daily, monthly, weekly, or yearly, is provided then the underlying scheduler implementation will use its features to decide when best to run.

documents.title: Option: providers.scheduler.tasks.<name>.user

documents.title: Option: security.pki.certificateFiles
documents.content: A list of files containing trusted root certificates in PEM format. These are concatenated to form {file}/etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt, which is used by many programs that use OpenSSL, such as {command}curl and {command}git.

documents.title: Option: security.pki.certificates
documents.content: A list of trusted root certificates in PEM format.

documents.title: Option: security.pki.useCompatibleBundle
documents.content:
Whether to enable usage of a compatibility bundle.
Such a bundle consists exclusively of BEGIN CERTIFICATE and no BEGIN TRUSTED CERTIFICATE, which is an OpenSSL specific PEM format.
It is known to be incompatible with certain software stacks.
Nevertheless, enabling this will strip all additional trust rules provided by the certificates themselves. This can have security consequences depending on your usecases .

documents.title: Option: security.wrapperDirSize
documents.content: Size limit for the /run/wrappers tmpfs. Look at {manpage}mount(8), tmpfs size option, for the accepted syntax. WARNING: don't set to less than 64MB.

documents.title: Option: security.wrappers
documents.content: This option effectively allows adding setuid/setgid bits, capabilities, changing file ownership and permissions of a program without directly modifying it. This works by creating a wrapper program under the {option}security.wrapperDir directory, which is then added to the shell PATH.

documents.title: Option: security.wrappers.<name>.capabilities
documents.content:
A comma-separated list of capability clauses to be given to the wrapper program. The format for capability clauses is described in the “TEXTUAL REPRESENTATION” section of the {manpage}cap_from_text(3) manual page. For a list of capabilities supported by the system, check the {manpage}capabilities(7) manual page.
::: {.note} cap_setpcap, which is required for the wrapper program to be able to raise caps into the Ambient set is NOT raised to the Ambient set so that the real program cannot modify its own capabilities!! This may be too restrictive for cases in which the real program needs cap_setpcap but it at least leans on the side security paranoid vs. too relaxed. :::

documents.title: Option: security.wrappers.<name>.enable

documents.title: Option: security.wrappers.<name>.permissions

documents.title: Option: security.wrappers.<name>.program

documents.title: Option: security.wrappers.<name>.setgid

documents.title: Option: services.anacron.settings.NO_MAIL_OUTPUT
documents.content: If defined (and non-empty), the standard output and error descriptors of job processes are not redirected and e-mailed.

documents.title: Option: services.anacron.settings.PREFERRED_HOUR
documents.content: Preferred hour of start of the job. If the hour is missed job will be skipped.

documents.title: Option: services.anacron.settings.RANDOM_DELAY
documents.content:
Allows delaying job startups by random amount of minutes with upper limit specified by this value. The random scaling factor is determined during the cron daemon startup so it remains constant for the whole run time of the daemon.
The maximum number of minutes that will be added to the delay in minutes variable which is specified for each job. A RANDOM_DELAY set to 12 would therefore add, randomly, between 0 and 12 minutes to the delay in minutes for each job in that particular anacrontab. When set to 0, no random delay is added.

documents.title: Option: services.anacron.settings.START_HOURS_RANGE
documents.content: An interval (in hours) when scheduled jobs can be run. In case this time interval is missed, for example, due to a power down, then scheduled jobs are not executed that day.

documents.title: Option: services.docker.prune.interval
documents.content:

If a standard {manpage}crontab(5) expression is provided this value will be passed directly to the scheduler implementation and execute exactly as specified.
If one of the special values, hourly, daily, monthly, weekly, or yearly, is provided then the underlying scheduler implementation will use its features to decide when best to run.

Whether to enable nix as a system service.
::: {.warning} Disabling nix makes the system hard to modify and the Nix programs and configuration will not be made available by NixOS itself. :::

documents.title: Option: services.nix-daemon.nrBuildUsers
documents.content: Number of nixbld user accounts created to perform secure concurrent builds. If you receive an error message saying that "all build users are currently in use", you should increase this value.

documents.title: Option: services.nix-daemon.package
documents.content: The package to use for nix.

documents.title: Option: services.nix-daemon.settings
documents.content:
Configuration for Nix, see https://nixos.org/manual/nix/stable/command-ref/conf-file.html or {manpage}nix.conf(5) for available options. The value declared here will be translated directly to the key-value pairs Nix expects.
You can use {command}nix-instantiate --eval --strict '<nixpkgs/nixos>' -A config.nix.settings to view the current value. By default it is empty.
Nix configurations defined under {option}nix.* will be translated and applied to this option. In addition, configuration specified in {option}nix.extraOptions will be appended verbatim to the resulting config file.

documents.title: Option: services.nix-daemon.settings.allowed-users
documents.content: A list of names of users (separated by whitespace) that are allowed to connect to the Nix daemon. As with {option}nix.settings.trusted-users, you can specify groups by prefixing them with @. Also, you can allow all users by specifying *. The default is *. Note that trusted users are always allowed to connect.

documents.title: Option: services.nix-daemon.settings.auto-optimise-store
documents.content: If set to true, Nix automatically detects files in the store that have identical contents, and replaces them with hard links to a single copy. This saves disk space. If set to false (the default), you can still run nix-store --optimise to get rid of duplicate files.

documents.title: Option: services.nix-daemon.settings.cores
documents.content: This option defines the maximum number of concurrent tasks during one build. It affects, e.g., -j option for make. The special value 0 means that the builder should use all available CPU cores in the system. Some builds may become non-deterministic with this option; use with care! Packages will only be affected if enableParallelBuilding is set for them.

documents.title: Option: services.nix-daemon.settings.max-jobs
documents.content: This option defines the maximum number of jobs that Nix will try to build in parallel. The default is auto, which means it will use all available logical cores. It is recommend to set it to the total number of logical cores in your system (e.g., 16 for two CPUs with 4 cores each and hyper-threading).

documents.title: Option: services.nix-daemon.settings.require-sigs
documents.content: If enabled (the default), Nix will only download binaries from binary caches if they are cryptographically signed with any of the keys listed in {option}nix.settings.trusted-public-keys. If disabled, signatures are neither required nor checked, so it's strongly recommended that you use only trustworthy caches and https to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks.

documents.title: Option: services.nix-daemon.settings.sandbox
documents.content:
If set, Nix will perform builds in a sandboxed environment that it will set up automatically for each build. This prevents impurities in builds by disallowing access to dependencies outside of the Nix store by using network and mount namespaces in a chroot environment.
This is enabled by default even though it has a possible performance impact due to the initial setup time of a sandbox for each build. It doesn't affect derivation hashes, so changing this option will not trigger a rebuild of packages.
When set to "relaxed", this option permits derivations that set __noChroot = true; to run outside of the sandboxed environment. Exercise caution when using this mode of operation! It is intended to be a quick hack when building with packages that are not easily setup to be built reproducibly.

documents.title: Option: services.nix-daemon.settings.substituters
documents.content:
List of binary cache URLs used to obtain pre-built binaries of Nix packages.
By default https://cache.nixos.org/ is added.

documents.title: Option: services.nix-daemon.settings.system-features
documents.content: The set of features supported by the machine. Derivations can express dependencies on system features through the requiredSystemFeatures attribute.

documents.title: Option: services.nix-daemon.settings.trusted-public-keys

documents.content:
The interval at which this task should run its specified {option}command. Accepts either a standard {manpage}crontab(5) expression or one of: hourly, daily, weekly, monthly, or yearly.
If a standard {manpage}crontab(5) expression is provided this value will be passed directly to the scheduler implementation and execute exactly as specified.

If a standard {manpage}crontab(5) expression is provided this value will be passed directly to the scheduler implementation and execute exactly as specified.

documents.title: Option: services.zfs.autoSnapshot.enable
documents.content:
Enable the (OpenSolaris-compatible) ZFS auto-snapshotting service. Note that you must set the com.sun:auto-snapshot property to true on all datasets which you wish to auto-snapshot.
You can override a child dataset to use, or not use auto-snapshotting by setting its flag with the given interval: zfs set com.sun:auto-snapshot:weekly=false DATASET

documents.title: Option: services.zfs.autoSnapshot.flags
documents.content:
Flags to pass to the zfs-auto-snapshot command.
Run zfs-auto-snapshot (without any arguments) to see available flags.
If it's not too inconvenient for snapshots to have timestamps in UTC, it is suggested that you append --utc to the list of default options (see example).
Otherwise, snapshot names can cause name conflicts or apparent time reversals due to daylight savings, timezone or other date/time changes.

documents.title: Option: users.users.<name>.isNormalUser
documents.content: Indicates whether this is an account for a “real” user. This automatically sets {option}group to users, {option}createHome to true, {option}home to {file}/home/«username», {option}shell to {option}users.defaultUserShell, and {option}isSystemUser to false. Exactly one of isNormalUser and isSystemUser must be true.

documents.title: Option: users.users.<name>.isSystemUser
documents.content: Indicates if the user is a system user or not. This option only has an effect if {option}uid is {option}null, in which case it determines whether the user's UID is allocated in the range for system users (below 1000) or in the range for normal users (starting at 1000). Exactly one of isNormalUser and isSystemUser must be true.

documents.title: Option: users.users.<name>.name
documents.content: The name of the user account. If undefined, the name of the attribute set will be used.

documents.title: Option: users.users.<name>.packages
documents.content: The set of packages that should be made available to the user. This is in contrast to {option}environment.systemPackages, which adds packages to all users.

documents.title: Option: users.users.<name>.password
documents.content: Specifies the hashed password for the user.

documents.title: Option: users.users.<name>.passwordFile
documents.content: The full path to a file that contains the hash of the user's password. The password file is read on each system activation. The file should contain exactly one line, which should be the password in an encrypted form that is suitable for the chpasswd -e command.

documents.title: Option: users.users.<name>.shell
documents.content: The path to the user's shell. Can use shell derivations, like pkgs.bashInteractive. Don't forget to enable your shell in programs if necessary, like programs.zsh.enable = true;.

documents.title: Option: users.users.<name>.uid
documents.content: The account UID. If the UID is null, a free UID is picked on activation.

documents.title: Option: xdg.autostart.enable
documents.content: Whether to install files to support the XDG Autostart specification.

documents.title: Option: xdg.icons.enable
documents.content: This option has no description.

documents.title: Option: xdg.mime.addedAssociations
documents.content: Adds associations between mimetypes and applications. See the specifications for more information.

documents.title: Option: xdg.mime.defaultApplications
documents.content: Sets the default applications for given mimetypes. See the specifications for more information.

documents.title: Option: xdg.mime.enable
documents.content: Whether to install files to support the XDG Shared MIME-info specification and the XDG MIME Applications specification.

documents.title: Option: xdg.mime.removedAssociations
documents.content: Removes associations between mimetypes and applications. See the specifications for more information.

documents.title: Option: xdg.portal.enable
documents.content: Whether to enable XDG desktop portals.

documents.title: Option: xdg.portal.package
documents.content: The package to use for xdg-desktop-portal.

documents.title: Option: xdg.portal.portals
documents.content: List of XDG desktop portal packages to install.

documents.title: Option: xdg.terminal-exec.enable
documents.content: Whether to enable xdg-terminal-exec, the proposed Default Terminal Execution Specification.

documents.title: Option: xdg.terminal-exec.package
documents.content: The xdg-terminal-exec package to use.

documents.title: Option: xdg.terminal-exec.settings
documents.content:
Configuration options for the Default Terminal Execution Specification.
The keys are the desktop environments that are matched (case-insensitively) against $XDG_CURRENT_DESKTOP, or default which is used when the current desktop environment is not found in the configuration. The values are a list of terminals' desktop file IDs to try in order of decreasing priority.
